molecular_genetics.title: Genome-wide association study of 14,000 cases of seven common diseases and 3,000 shared controls
molecular_genetics.abstract: There is increasing evidence that genome-wide association (GWA) studies represent a powerful approach to the identification of genes involved in common human diseases. We describe a joint GWA study (using the Affymetrix GeneChip 500K Mapping Array Set) undertaken in the British population, which has examined approximately 2,000 individuals for each of 7 major diseases and a shared set of approximately 3,000 controls. Case collections were drawn from coronary artery disease, type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn's disease, bipolar disorder, and hypertension. We report strong association signals for type 1 diabetes at multiple loci including HLA, PTPN22, CTLA4, and IL2RA. For type 2 diabetes, we confirm TCF7L2, CDKAL1, CDKN2A/B, IGF2BP2, HHEX-IDE, SLC30A8, and FTO. For Crohn's disease, significant association was found at NOD2, IL23R, ATG16L1, and a gene desert on chromosome 5p13.1. For coronary artery disease, a strong signal was observed at chromosome 9p21.3. For rheumatoid arthritis, we identified associations at HLA-DRB1 and PTPN22. For bipolar disorder, a signal at 16p12 was observed. For hypertension, no strong signals achieved genome-wide significance. This study has demonstrated that careful analysis of common variation through large-scale genome-wide association can identify robustly replicating disease susceptibility loci for multiple common complex diseases.

molecular_genetics.title: Comprehensive molecular portraits of human breast tumours
molecular_genetics.abstract: We analysed primary breast cancers by genomic DNA copy number arrays, DNA methylation, exome sequencing, messenger RNA arrays, microRNA sequencing and reverse-phase protein arrays. Our ability to integrate information across platforms provided key insights into previously defined gene expression subtypes and demonstrated the existence of four main breast cancer classes when combining data from five platforms, each of which shows significant molecular heterogeneity. Somatic mutations in only three genes (TP53, PIK3CA and GATA3) occurred at greater than 10% incidence across all breast cancers; however, there were numerous subtype-associated and novel gene mutations including the enrichment of specific mutations in GATA3, PIK3CA and MAP3K1 with the luminal A subtype. We identified two novel protein-expression-defined subgroups, possibly produced by stromal and microenvironmental elements, and integrated analyses identified specific signalling pathways dominant in each molecular subtype including a HER2/phosphorylated HER2/EGFR/phosphorylated EGFR signature within the HER2-enriched expression subtype. Comparison of basal-like breast tumours with high-grade serous ovarian tumours showed many molecular commonalities, indicating a shared aetiology and similar therapeutic opportunities. The biological finding of the four main breast cancer subtypes caused by different subsets of genetic and epigenetic abnormalities raises the hypothesis that much of the clinically observable plasticity and heterogeneity occurs within, and not across, parsing of the four main biological subtypes of breast cancer.

molecular_genetics.title: RNA-Seq analysis of differential gene expression in liver from lactating dairy cows divergent in negative energy balance
molecular_genetics.abstract: Negative energy balance (NEB) is an important metabolic state in high-producing dairy cows during early lactation, where energy expenditure for milk production exceeds energy intake. We used RNA-Seq to investigate differential gene expression in liver tissue from dairy cows in severe NEB versus mild NEB during the early postpartum period. Liver biopsies were collected at 14 days postpartum from cows divergent in energy balance status. RNA was extracted and sequenced on the Illumina HiSeq 2000 platform, generating approximately 25 million paired-end reads per sample. A total of 782 genes were differentially expressed (adjusted P < 0.05, fold change > 1.5) between severe NEB and mild NEB cows. Pathway analysis revealed enrichment in fatty acid metabolism (CPT1A, ACOX1, HMGCS2 upregulated 3.2-fold, 2.8-fold, and 4.1-fold respectively), gluconeogenesis (PCK1 upregulated 2.5-fold, PCK2 upregulated 1.9-fold), and hepatic lipidosis pathways. Genes involved in the inflammatory response, including SAA3 (8.7-fold increase), HP (5.3-fold increase), and TNF (2.1-fold increase), were significantly upregulated in severe NEB. Conversely, genes associated with cholesterol biosynthesis (HMGCR, SQLE) were downregulated. These findings provide a comprehensive transcriptomic landscape of the hepatic response to NEB and identify candidate genes and pathways that may serve as biomarkers for metabolic health in dairy cattle.

molecular_genetics.title: Estimation of the warfarin dose with clinical and pharmacogenomic data
molecular_genetics.abstract: Warfarin is the most widely used oral anticoagulant, but dosing is complicated by wide interindividual variability. We developed a pharmacogenomic dosing algorithm using clinical variables and genotype information for CYP2C9 and VKORC1. A total of 4,043 patients from multiple sites worldwide were enrolled. Genotyping was performed for CYP2C9 (*2 and *3 alleles) and VKORC1 (-1639G>A). A multiple regression model incorporating age, body surface area, target INR, amiodarone use, CYP2C9 genotype, and VKORC1 genotype explained approximately 47% of the variability in therapeutic warfarin dose, compared with 17% for a clinical-only model and 12% for a fixed-dose approach. Patients carrying CYP2C9*2 or *3 variants required 18-37% lower doses than wild-type patients. The VKORC1 -1639 A/A genotype was associated with a 28% dose reduction compared with G/G. The pharmacogenomic algorithm significantly outperformed both clinical and fixed-dose approaches (P < 0.001) in accurately predicting the therapeutic dose, particularly for patients requiring unusually high or low doses. Among patients requiring 21 mg per week or less, the pharmacogenomic algorithm identified 48.4% of patients in the correct dose range, versus 33.1% for the clinical algorithm. These results demonstrate that pharmacogenomic-guided warfarin dosing can improve dose prediction and potentially reduce adverse events during anticoagulation therapy initiation.

molecular_genetics.title: Exome sequencing identifies the cause of a mendelian disorder
molecular_genetics.abstract: We demonstrate the power of exome sequencing to identify the genetic basis of a rare Mendelian disorder. We studied a family with Miller syndrome (postaxial acrofacial dysostosis), a rare autosomal recessive disorder characterized by craniofacial malformations including cleft lip and palate, malar hypoplasia, and limb defects including absence or hypoplasia of the fifth ray and postaxial elements. We sequenced the exomes of four affected individuals in a consanguineous kindred using targeted capture and massively parallel sequencing on the Illumina Genome Analyzer platform. After filtering against public SNP databases (dbSNP, HapMap) and eight HapMap exomes, we identified mutations in DHODH, which encodes dihydroorotate dehydrogenase, a key enzyme in the pyrimidine de novo biosynthesis pathway. Two compound heterozygous mutations (c.403C>T, p.R135C and c.1036G>A, p.G346R) were identified, each segregating with disease in the family. Both mutations affect highly conserved residues and are predicted to be damaging by PolyPhen and SIFT algorithms. Functional studies in yeast complementation assays confirmed that both mutations significantly reduce enzymatic activity to less than 20% of wild-type levels. We subsequently identified DHODH mutations in two additional unrelated families with Miller syndrome. This study illustrates that exome sequencing of a small number of affected individuals can identify causative genes for rare Mendelian disorders and opens new avenues for understanding fundamental biological pathways disrupted in developmental malformations.

irrelevant.title: Psychological interventions for diabetes-related distress in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus
irrelevant.abstract: Diabetes-related distress affects approximately 36% of adults with type 2 diabetes and is associated with reduced self-management, poorer glycaemic control, and decreased quality of life. We conducted a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials evaluating psychological interventions for diabetes-related distress. We searched MEDLINE, Embase, PsycINFO, CINAHL, and the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials from inception to March 2017. Eligible studies were RCTs comparing psychological interventions (cognitive behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based interventions, motivational interviewing, or psychoeducation) with usual care or active comparators in adults with type 2 diabetes. Two reviewers independently screened citations, extracted data, and assessed risk of bias using the Cochrane tool. Thirty studies (n = 4,760 participants) met inclusion criteria. Meta-analysis showed psychological interventions significantly reduced diabetes distress (standardized mean difference -0.34, 95% CI -0.47 to -0.21, P < 0.001) and HbA1c levels (mean difference -0.29%, 95% CI -0.43 to -0.15) compared with usual care. Cognitive behavioral therapy showed the largest effect sizes. Most studies had moderate risk of bias due to inadequate blinding. The quality of evidence was moderate overall according to GRADE assessment. Psychological interventions appear effective for reducing diabetes-related distress and improving glycaemic outcomes in adults with type 2 diabetes, but further high-quality trials with longer follow-up are warranted.

irrelevant.title: Effectiveness of exercise interventions on falls prevention in ambulatory community-dwelling older adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials
irrelevant.abstract: Falls are a leading cause of injury-related morbidity and mortality in older adults, with approximately one-third of community-dwelling adults aged 65 and over falling each year. We performed a systematic review and meta-analysis to evaluate the effectiveness of exercise-based interventions in preventing falls among community-dwelling older adults. Electronic databases including MEDLINE, Embase, CINAHL, SPORTDiscus, and the Cochrane Library were searched from inception to December 2018. Randomized controlled trials comparing structured exercise programs (balance training, strength training, tai chi, or multicomponent programs) with control conditions in community-dwelling adults aged 60 years and older were included. Fifty-nine trials involving 15,294 participants met inclusion criteria. Exercise interventions reduced the rate of falls by 23% (rate ratio 0.77, 95% CI 0.71 to 0.83, P < 0.001) and the number of fallers by 15% (risk ratio 0.85, 95% CI 0.79 to 0.91). Programs that included balance and functional exercises were more effective than those focused solely on resistance or aerobic training. Higher dose programs (more than 3 hours per week) showed greater benefits. Tai chi programs demonstrated significant fall rate reduction (rate ratio 0.72, 95% CI 0.59 to 0.88). The evidence quality was high for overall falls reduction. Exercise programs, particularly those incorporating balance training and delivered at sufficient intensity, effectively reduce falls in community-dwelling older adults.

irrelevant.title: Conservative versus liberal approach to fluid therapy in septic shock: a randomized clinical trial
irrelevant.abstract: Fluid resuscitation is a cornerstone of septic shock management, but the optimal volume strategy remains debated. We conducted a multicenter, randomized, parallel-group clinical trial comparing a conservative fluid strategy with a liberal fluid strategy in adults with septic shock admitted to intensive care units. Patients were randomly assigned within 12 hours of septic shock recognition to a conservative strategy (fluid boluses only if serum lactate was above 4 mmol/L and central venous oxygen saturation below 70%) or a liberal strategy (fluid boluses for any signs of hypoperfusion including tachycardia, hypotension, or oliguria). The primary outcome was 90-day all-cause mortality. A total of 1,554 patients were enrolled across 32 ICUs in nine countries. Mean cumulative fluid volumes at 5 days were 3,750 mL in the conservative group and 6,280 mL in the liberal group (difference 2,530 mL, P < 0.001). The 90-day mortality was 40.2% in the conservative group versus 42.8% in the liberal group (adjusted relative risk 0.94, 95% CI 0.84 to 1.05, P = 0.26). The conservative group had significantly fewer days on mechanical ventilation (median 3 vs 5 days, P = 0.003) and less acute kidney injury requiring renal replacement therapy (21% vs 28%, P = 0.01). Serious adverse events including ischemic events were similar between groups. While 90-day mortality did not differ significantly, a conservative fluid approach in septic shock reduced organ support requirements.

irrelevant.title: Effect of high-dose vitamin D supplementation on volumetric bone density and bone strength: a randomized clinical trial
irrelevant.abstract: Vitamin D supplementation is widely recommended for bone health, but the effects of different doses on bone density and microarchitecture remain uncertain. We conducted a double-blind, randomized clinical trial to compare the effects of three doses of vitamin D3 on volumetric bone mineral density (vBMD) and bone strength over 3 years. Community-dwelling adults aged 55 to 70 years with baseline 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels between 30 and 125 nmol/L were randomly assigned to receive 400 IU (n = 109), 4,000 IU (n = 100), or 10,000 IU (n = 102) of vitamin D3 daily. The primary outcome was change in total vBMD at the radius, measured by high-resolution peripheral quantitative computed tomography. At 3 years, the group receiving 400 IU had a mean change in radial total vBMD of -1.2% (95% CI -1.7 to -0.6), the 4,000 IU group had -2.4% (95% CI -3.1 to -1.7), and the 10,000 IU group had -3.5% (95% CI -4.2 to -2.8, P < 0.001 for trend). Similar dose-dependent decreases were observed at the tibia. Finite element analysis showed corresponding decreases in estimated bone strength. Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels increased in a dose-dependent manner. Higher doses of vitamin D supplementation resulted in lower, not higher, volumetric bone density over 3 years, suggesting that high-dose vitamin D supplementation may not benefit skeletal health.

irrelevant.title: Effectiveness of nurse-led self-management education on heart failure patients' self-care, knowledge, and symptom management: a randomized controlled trial
irrelevant.abstract: Heart failure is a chronic condition requiring extensive self-management, yet many patients lack adequate knowledge and skills for effective self-care. This study evaluated the effectiveness of a structured nurse-led self-management education program on self-care behaviors, disease knowledge, and symptom management in heart failure patients. A two-group randomized controlled trial was conducted at three tertiary hospitals from January 2014 to June 2015. A total of 186 patients with NYHA class II-III heart failure were randomly assigned to intervention (n = 93) or usual care (n = 93) groups. The intervention group received a 12-week structured education program delivered by trained cardiac nurses, consisting of four face-to-face sessions, weekly telephone follow-ups, and a patient workbook covering medication adherence, dietary sodium restriction, daily weight monitoring, physical activity, and symptom recognition. Outcomes were measured using the Self-Care of Heart Failure Index, the Dutch Heart Failure Knowledge Scale, and a symptom diary at baseline, 12 weeks, and 6 months. The intervention group showed significantly greater improvement in self-care maintenance (mean difference 14.3, 95% CI 9.8 to 18.7, P < 0.001), self-care management (mean difference 11.6, P < 0.001), and heart failure knowledge (mean difference 3.2, P < 0.001) compared with the control group. Benefits were sustained at 6 months. Hospital readmission rates were lower in the intervention group (18.3% vs 31.2%, P = 0.04). Nurse-led self-management education significantly improves heart failure patients' self-care behaviors and may reduce healthcare utilization.

irrelevant.title: Impact of structured communication tools on patient safety outcomes in acute care nursing: a cluster randomized trial
irrelevant.abstract: Communication failures are a leading cause of adverse events in hospitals. We evaluated the impact of implementing structured communication tools, specifically the SBAR (Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation) protocol and standardized handoff checklists, on patient safety outcomes in acute care nursing units. A cluster randomized trial was conducted across 24 medical-surgical units in six hospitals from March 2016 to February 2018. Units were randomized to the intervention (structured SBAR training with pocket reference cards and electronic handoff templates, n = 12 units) or usual care (n = 12 units). Primary outcomes were rates of adverse events (medication errors, falls, pressure injuries, and failure-to-rescue episodes) per 1,000 patient days. Secondary outcomes included nurse-reported communication quality, job satisfaction, and handoff duration. A total of 4,832 nurses participated across 186,000 patient days. Adverse event rates were significantly lower in intervention units (12.4 vs 18.7 per 1,000 patient days, incidence rate ratio 0.66, 95% CI 0.54 to 0.81, P < 0.001). Medication error rates decreased by 31% and fall rates by 24% in intervention units. Nurses in the intervention group reported significantly higher communication quality scores (mean 4.1 vs 3.4 on a 5-point scale, P < 0.001) and greater confidence in clinical handoffs. Handoff duration was reduced by an average of 3.2 minutes per transfer. Implementation of structured communication tools in acute care nursing significantly improves patient safety and nursing communication quality.

irrelevant.title: Cost-effectiveness of total hip arthroplasty versus conservative management for osteoarthritis of the hip: a decision-analytic model
irrelevant.abstract: Total hip arthroplasty (THA) is an effective treatment for end-stage hip osteoarthritis, but its cost-effectiveness compared with continued conservative management remains relevant for resource allocation decisions. We developed a Markov decision-analytic model to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of THA versus conservative management (physiotherapy, analgesics, and weight management) in patients aged 60 to 80 years with moderate-to-severe hip osteoarthritis from a healthcare payer perspective over a lifetime horizon. Transition probabilities, utility values, and costs were derived from published literature, national joint registries, and hospital administrative databases. The model incorporated states for pre-operative, post-operative (with and without revision), conservative management, and death. Costs were expressed in 2018 US dollars with a 3% annual discount rate applied to both costs and quality-adjusted life years (QALYs). THA was associated with a mean lifetime cost of $42,600 compared with $28,400 for conservative management, and produced 11.8 QALYs compared with 9.3 QALYs, yielding an incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) of $5,680 per QALY gained. Probabilistic sensitivity analysis showed THA was cost-effective in 94% of simulations at a willingness-to-pay threshold of $50,000 per QALY. Results were robust across age subgroups, with ICERs ranging from $3,200 per QALY for patients aged 60-65 to $14,800 per QALY for patients aged 75-80. THA is a highly cost-effective intervention for hip osteoarthritis across a range of patient ages and model assumptions.

irrelevant.title: Economic evaluation of community-based screening for chronic kidney disease: a model-based cost-utility analysis
irrelevant.abstract: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) affects approximately 10% of the global population and imposes substantial economic burden through progression to end-stage renal disease requiring dialysis or transplantation. We conducted a cost-utility analysis to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of community-based screening programs for CKD in adults aged 50 to 75 years with hypertension or diabetes. A microsimulation model was developed following individual patient trajectories through CKD stages 1-5, dialysis, transplantation, and death over a 30-year time horizon from a societal perspective. Three strategies were compared: no screening, one-time screening with urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (ACR), and annual screening with ACR. Treatment effects of early angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor initiation in screen-detected CKD were modeled based on published trial data. Costs included screening, diagnostic workup, treatment, dialysis, and transplantation costs in 2019 US dollars, discounted at 3% annually. One-time screening produced an ICER of $22,400 per QALY gained versus no screening, while annual screening had an ICER of $38,700 per QALY versus one-time screening. At a willingness-to-pay threshold of $50,000 per QALY, both screening strategies were cost-effective. One-time screening prevented 8.4 cases of end-stage renal disease per 1,000 persons screened over 30 years. Annual screening prevented 12.1 cases per 1,000 screened. Community-based CKD screening in high-risk adults is likely cost-effective and could reduce the burden of end-stage renal disease.

irrelevant.title: Global, regional, and national incidence, prevalence, and years lived with disability for 328 diseases and injuries in 195 countries, 1990-2016: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2016
irrelevant.abstract: Measuring the incidence, prevalence, and years lived with disability (YLDs) for diseases and injuries is essential for understanding population health. As part of the Global Burden of Disease Study 2016, we estimated these metrics for 328 causes in 195 countries and territories from 1990 to 2016. Data were gathered from vital registration systems, disease registries, health surveys, published literature, and hospital discharge records. DisMod-MR 2.1, a Bayesian meta-regression tool, was used to generate internally consistent estimates. In 2016, there were 1.11 billion (95% uncertainty interval 1.07 to 1.15 billion) incident cases of short-duration conditions and 2.17 billion (2.07 to 2.27 billion) prevalent cases of long-duration conditions globally. Low back pain was the leading cause of YLDs globally in 2016, followed by migraine, age-related hearing loss, iron-deficiency anaemia, and major depressive disorder. Global age-standardized YLD rates remained relatively stable between 1990 and 2016, decreasing by only 2.7%. However, total YLDs increased by 53.7% due to population growth and ageing. Non-communicable diseases accounted for 80.8% of all YLDs in 2016. Regional variation was substantial, with the highest age-standardized YLD rates in low-income countries. These estimates highlight the growing burden of non-communicable diseases and the persistent challenge of chronic conditions as the global population ages and demographic transitions continue across regions.

irrelevant.title: Prevalence, incidence, and risk factors of type 2 diabetes mellitus in a large population-based cohort: the Rotterdam Study
irrelevant.abstract: Understanding the burden and determinants of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is critical for public health planning. We examined the prevalence, incidence, and modifiable risk factors for T2DM in the Rotterdam Study, a prospective population-based cohort of adults aged 45 years and older in the Netherlands. A total of 14,926 participants were enrolled between 1990 and 2014 and followed for a median of 11.2 years. T2DM was ascertained through fasting glucose measurements, oral glucose tolerance tests, medication records, and general practitioner registries. Age-standardized prevalence of T2DM at baseline was 11.2% (95% CI 10.7 to 11.8) in men and 9.1% (95% CI 8.6 to 9.5) in women. The overall age-standardized incidence rate was 8.3 per 1,000 person-years (95% CI 7.8 to 8.8). In multivariable Cox regression analysis, significant independent risk factors for incident T2DM included body mass index (hazard ratio 1.57 per 5 kg/m2 increase, 95% CI 1.45 to 1.70), waist circumference (HR 1.43 per 10 cm increase), family history of diabetes (HR 1.85, 95% CI 1.62 to 2.12), hypertension (HR 1.42), low physical activity (HR 1.31), and smoking (HR 1.28 for current smokers). A combined risk score incorporating these factors showed good discrimination (C-statistic 0.78). Approximately 52% of incident T2DM cases were attributable to modifiable risk factors. This large population-based study confirms the substantial burden of T2DM in European adults and demonstrates that a majority of new cases could potentially be prevented through lifestyle modification targeting obesity, physical inactivity, and smoking.
